2026-05-04 Meeting Notes

May 5  #897  
Christian Herber
  • All Messages By This Member

Hi all,

 

Minutes from yesterdayвҖҷs meeting:

Quick Recap

This RISC-V technical committee meeting focused on discussing interrupt architecture proposals, with Christian Herber presenting assembly code for a fast interrupt dispatcher and receiving feedback from Alexey and Evgenii on their alternative approach using an enhanced interrupt controller. The discussion centered on two main proposals: Christian's design requiring new CSRs and a loop overhead of approximately 12-13 instructions, and Evgenii and Alexey's alternative approach that moves more functionality to the interrupt controller itself. The group debated the merits of maintaining RISC-V's two-level interrupt hierarchy versus simplifying to a single-level approach, with concerns raised about hardware implementation complexity, CSR access requirements, and performance implications when dealing with external interrupt controllers operating at different frequencies. The conversation ended with agreement to continue the discussion offline and potentially revisit the proposals in more detail at the next meeting.

Next Steps

  • Evgenii: Send the alternative interrupt controller code snippet and approach to the mailing list for offline review by group members.

Summary

RISC-V Interrupt Handling Discussion:

Christian welcomed attendees to a RISC-V meeting and outlined key policies including community guidelines, antitrust compliance, and export controls. The discussion began with Christian reviewing the agenda, which included feedback on requirements for interrupt handling in Zephyr OS, specifically regarding the priority ordering between direct and regular interrupts. Christian noted that while direct interrupts might logically have higher priority than regular interrupts, he sought input from Alexej on this priority ordering, though no specific concerns were mentioned in the transcript.

 

Interrupt Priority Implementation Discussion:

Alexey explained that the easiest way to implement the desired functionality would be to switch priority between regular and direct interrupts by making direct interrupts higher priority. Christian agreed with this approach, though noted that some operating systems like Zephyr and Autosar do have this functionality. The discussion concluded with Christian mentioning his work on creating an assembly version of the handler to count instructions and optimize performance, including some bit shuffling that provided speed improvements.

 

Handler Assembly Code Presentation:

Christian presented an assembly version of the handler pseudo code, explaining the interrupt dispatch process and the use of a vector table for handling interrupts. He detailed the critical dispatch loop, which determines whether an interrupt is machine external or internal, and described how the code efficiently loads and calls the appropriate handler. Christian noted that the longest sequence with interrupts disabled consists of five instructions, and the overhead between interrupts is 12-13 instructions, which is less than the overhead of saving and restoring the entire context. No specific questions were raised during the presentation.

 

Interrupt Handling Code Discussion:

The team discussed a code snippet related to interrupt handling in a system. Christian explained how the code manages fast interrupts and regular interrupts, clarifying that regular interrupts can be preempted by other regular interrupts. Alexey raised concerns about the number of new registers required, but Christian clarified that only two new CSRs are needed for this specific functionality. The discussion also touched on the potential for optimizing the code using future instructions.

 

Interrupt Handling Optimization Proposal:

The team discussed performance optimization approaches for interrupt handling, with Alexey and Evgenii presenting a new approach that moves interrupt handling logic to an external interrupt controller rather than keeping it entirely on the core side. They explained that this approach reduces CSR accesses and minimizes interaction with the interrupt controller, potentially improving performance significantly. Christian requested to review the detailed code offline and suggested scheduling a follow-up meeting to present the complete proposal, including details about major interrupt redirection and other technical aspects that may not have been fully understood by the team.

 

Two-Level Hierarchy Design Challenges:

Christian and Alexey discussed challenges with the two-level hierarchy design, with Alexey arguing that Christian's approach doesn't allow efficient implementation for their scenarios. Christian acknowledged the group's preference for maintaining the two-level hierarchy but proposed a new register design that could combine top I and top external interrupt functions to optimize the dispatch loop. Christian suggested continuing the discussion on the mailing list and potentially addressing it in the next meeting.

 

Interrupt Handling Implementation Discussion:

The team discussed implementing interrupt handling, focusing on hardware vs software approaches and clock frequency considerations. Alexey raised concerns about using values from different frequency sources, while Christian argued that the existing problem with mtopi already exists in AIA and can be addressed through proper implementation. Evgenii emphasized the need to compete with ARM and NVIC while avoiding design complexity, suggesting a single source of truth for interrupt ID would simplify the design.

 

Regards,

 

Christian

  • #897
  • next page

RISC-VжҠҖжңҜе§”е‘ҳдјҡдјҡи®®иҒҡз„Ұдёӯж–ӯжһ¶жһ„жҸҗжЎҲпјҡChristian Herberеұ•зӨәеҝ«йҖҹдёӯж–ӯи°ғеәҰеҷЁжұҮзј–д»Јз ҒпјҢйңҖж–°еўһCSRдё”еҫӘзҺҜејҖй”Җ12-13жқЎжҢҮд»ӨпјӣEvgeniiдёҺAlexeyжҸҗеҮәе°ҶеҠҹиғҪз§»иҮідёӯж–ӯжҺ§еҲ¶еҷЁзҡ„жӣҝд»Јж–№жЎҲгҖӮеҸҢж–№дәүи®әдёӨзә§дёҺеҚ•зә§дёӯж–ӯеұӮж¬ЎдјҳеҠЈпјҢе…іжіЁзЎ¬д»¶еӨҚжқӮеәҰгҖҒCSRи®ҝй—®еҸҠеӨ–йғЁдёӯж–ӯжҺ§еҲ¶еҷЁйў‘зҺҮе·®ејӮгҖӮз»“и®әпјҡз»§з»ӯзәҝдёӢи®Ёи®әпјҢEvgeniiе°ҶйӮ®д»¶еҸ‘йҖҒжӣҝд»Јж–№жЎҲд»Јз ҒгҖӮ
